title: How Amsterdam invented the fire department - Works in Progress Magazine
gist: Seventeenth-century Amsterdam cut fire losses to under 1% of prior levels not by inventing a single machine but by pairing Jan van der Heyden's hose-based fire engine with a redesigned institution: district companies, a citywide alarm network, and fines-and-rewards incentives, in an early example of what would later be called systems analysis. The case shows how a coordination problem was solved decades before fire insurance existed, through state capacity and incentive design rather than markets.

themes.theme: Wealth as an amplifier of urban fire risk

insights.claim: Rising material abundance in a seventeenth-century commercial city directly increased fire risk, as cheaper textiles like curtains near candles became a common ignition source that spread to wooden interiors.
insights.speaker: WorksInProgMag
insights.so_what: Prosperity and fire-load rise together; modern analogue is how densification and new materials (lithium batteries, data centers) create novel catastrophic risks that outpace existing safety regimes.
insights.quote: Curtains became far more common: easily set alight by a carelessly positioned candle, they were the source of many fires that then spread to wooden furniture and walls
insights.locator: People of all ranks owned more clothes and furnishings

insights.claim: Concentrations of industrial inputs magnified systemic fire risk: maintaining a shipping fleet required stores of highly combustible pitch, tar, turpentine, and hemp, while brewing, printing, resin, sugar, soap, and sawmilling all added their own hazards.
insights.speaker: WorksInProgMag
insights.so_what: Clustered flammable inventory is a correlated-risk problem; the same logic underlies why insurers and regulators scrutinize concentration in warehousing, chemical storage, and grid infrastructure today.
insights.locator: If its homes were vulnerable, Amsterdam's factories, workshops, and piers were in even greater peril

insights.claim: Despite heightened hazards, Amsterdam avoided a catastrophe like London's Great Fire of 1666, and fire losses fell dramatically over the century's last three decades due to a culture of inventiveness combined with what would today be called state capacity.
insights.speaker: WorksInProgMag
insights.so_what: The central claim: outcomes diverged not from luck but from institutional capacity to build and operate systems; a durable lens for comparing cities/states on disaster resilience.
insights.locator: Yet Amsterdam avoided the disastrous equivalent of London's Great Fire of 1666

themes.theme: The Hautsch engines: capital without capability

insights.claim: By 1670 Amsterdam had deployed roughly 60 horse-dragged water-pumping engines bought from Nuremberg inventor Hans Hautsch, plus ladders, hooks, tarpaulins, and more than 28,000 leather buckets, staffed by members of four guilds — making it the largest and best-equipped firefighting system in Europe.
insights.speaker: WorksInProgMag
insights.so_what: Being the best-resourced player is not the same as being effective; capex leadership can mask a capability gap that only failure exposes.
insights.locator: In the 1660s, the city purchased dozens of large water-pumping engines

insights.claim: The Hautsch engines' fatal limitation was hydraulic reach: their rigid nozzled pipes could only spray the exterior and lower floors, so firefighters could not deliver water to a roof or deep into a building to quench a fire at its origin.
insights.speaker: WorksInProgMag
insights.so_what: A binding technical constraint, not resource scarcity, was the true bottleneck — a reminder to diagnose the specific failure mode before scaling spend.
insights.quote: The streams of water sprayed only the outside of the building and the lower levels
insights.locator: The essential problem was that fire fighters couldn't get enough water to the roof

insights.claim: The December 1669 sugar-refinery fire on the Laurier Canal destroyed an estimated 195,000 guilders (perhaps $20 million today) with the owner uninsured, since fire insurance was still almost a century away, and the ruined owner died days later.
insights.speaker: WorksInProgMag
insights.so_what: Before insurance, catastrophic loss was total and personal; the absence of risk-transfer markets raised the social payoff to public loss-prevention infrastructure.
insights.locator: On a December night in 1669, a large, new sugar refinery on the Laurier Canal caught fire

insights.claim: The 1672 fire at Joan Blaeu's printing office — then the largest in the world and home to the Atlas maior plates — caused 382,000 guilders in losses because the building was far from any canal and freezing temperatures rendered the water pumps useless, and Blaeu never recovered and died the following year.
insights.speaker: WorksInProgMag
insights.so_what: Environmental edge cases (distance from water, freezing) destroy systems designed only for average conditions; robustness to tail conditions is where value is won or lost.
insights.locator: Even more devastating was a 1672 fire

themes.theme: Van der Heyden and systems-level invention

insights.claim: The decisive innovator, Jan van der Heyden, was a celebrated cityscape painter (the 'Dutch Canaletto') who was simultaneously a prolific inventor and systems manager, later chronicling Amsterdam's fires in a 1690 account co-written with his son.
insights.speaker: WorksInProgMag
insights.so_what: Breakthrough operators often come from adjacent, non-obvious fields; talent scouting that over-indexes on domain credentials misses cross-disciplinary system-builders.
insights.locator: Van der Heyden was a painter famous for his meticulously detailed city scenes

insights.claim: Before tackling fire, Van der Heyden built Amsterdam's street-lighting system of 1,800 purpose-designed oil lanterns installed by 1670, earning appointment as 'director of the municipal lights' at 2,000 guilders a year and making the city the best-lit in Europe.
insights.speaker: WorksInProgMag
insights.so_what: The same operator delivering a prior public-infrastructure success bought the credibility and municipal relationships that made later fire reforms adoptable — track records compound across projects.
insights.locator: In 1669, he proposed a plan to light Amsterdam's streets with 1,800 oil lanterns

insights.claim: The Van der Heyden brothers' new fire engine solved the reach problem with three linked features: a leather suction hose to draw water continuously from a distance, a flexible leather 'snake' hose to aim deep inside buildings, and an air chamber delivering a continuous high-pressure stream that also resisted freezing.
insights.speaker: WorksInProgMag
insights.so_what: The winning design attacked exactly the failure modes (reach, continuity, freezing) that had destroyed the refinery and Blaeu's shop — targeted redesign around observed failures beats incremental improvement.
insights.locator: There were three critical elements to the brothers' new fire engines

insights.claim: Van der Heyden's distinction was that he designed not just a machine but how it would operate in practice, an approach a translator characterized as one of the earliest instances of the method now called systems analysis.
insights.speaker: WorksInProgMag
insights.so_what: The transferable framework: durable innovation optimizes the whole operating system, not the device — the same insight separates deployed AI/tech from lab demos today.
insights.quote: one of the earliest instances of a method now called systems analysis
insights.locator: Jan van der Heyden was not just an ingenious mechanic like Hans Hautsch

themes.theme: Adoption friction and the turning-point fire

insights.claim: Even after the city council retrofitted half its engines in November 1672, adoption stalled because at a January harbor fire the lone new engine arrived late and firefighters, amid crisis, refused to risk 'new and unproven things.'
insights.speaker: WorksInProgMag
insights.so_what: Superior technology stalls on operator risk-aversion under pressure; adoption is a behavioral and organizational problem, not just a performance one.
insights.quote: The fire fighters did not think that this was the time to try new and unproven things
insights.locator: The lone new engine arrived late

insights.claim: The same harbor fire became the turning point when the single new engine, put reluctantly to work on a blaze thought unwinnable, extinguished about a hundred feet of burning rubble and saved everything left standing, making it the last fire ever fought with Amsterdam's unmodified old engines.
insights.speaker: WorksInProgMag
insights.so_what: A single vivid, verifiable demonstration flipped institutional adoption — the pattern for how disruptive tools cross the chasm once proof beats skepticism.
insights.quote: A single new engine, coming late and finally reluctantly put to work on what was thought to be unavoidably lost, had preserved everything that had still been standing
insights.locator: This incident showed clear as day the difference between the two engines

insights.claim: Full institutional rollout took nearly a decade: only in 1681 did the mayors order the Van der Heydens to supply engines to each of the city's sixty districts and to draw up regulations, after users themselves petitioned in 1679 to swap awkward retrofitted engines for lighter new ones.
insights.speaker: WorksInProgMag
insights.so_what: Institutional and organizational change lags the technology by years; the demand pull came from front-line users, not top-down mandate — a governance pattern worth watching in any tech deployment.
insights.locator: The transition to new technology, and the organizational changes to back it up, took time

themes.theme: Incentive design, results, and political economy

insights.claim: The reformed fire service was engineered around market-style incentives: watchmen who failed to notify firefighters paid the firefighters' fines, householders who fought a fire before sounding the alarm were fined, and the first three companies reaching a fire received monetary awards.
insights.speaker: WorksInProgMag
insights.so_what: A coordination problem was solved by aligning individual payoffs with speed and reporting — the same mechanism-design logic behind modern insurance deductibles, bounties, and SLA penalties.
insights.locator: The new system 'was honeycombed with market incentives'

insights.claim: Firefighting was structured as compulsory civic duty rather than paid employment: each district company comprised 36 residents under two fire masters, and any man appointed had to serve a year or pay a ten-guilder fine.
insights.speaker: WorksInProgMag
insights.so_what: Public safety was financed through mandated service backed by opt-out fines rather than salaries — an institutional design that lowered fiscal cost while ensuring staffing.
insights.locator: Fire fighters were not paid salaries, however

insights.claim: Under the new system, Amsterdam suffered 41 fires from 1682 to 1687 with total losses of just 18,353 guilders — less than one percent of the losses recorded from 1670 to 1681 under the old system.
insights.speaker: WorksInProgMag
insights.so_what: A two-orders-of-magnitude drop in loss quantifies the payoff of combining new technology with redesigned institutions — the kind of before/after evidence investors should demand of any operational overhaul.

insights.claim: The reforms succeeded because they fit Amsterdam's political economy: merchant-family mayors with broadly based interests actively promoted 'useful' inventions, and citizens willingly paid high taxes believing they got their money's worth.
insights.speaker: WorksInProgMag
insights.so_what: Innovation sticks where the governing coalition's incentives and civic trust support public investment — the enabling condition to look for when betting on state-capacity-dependent projects.
insights.locator: It mattered, too, that Van der Heyden's inventions and reforms suited the ethos and political economy of his city
